sexual_orientation.definition_in_act: Sexuality - includes heterosexuality, homosexuality and bisexuality

gender_identity.definition_in_act: Gender identity - means the gender-related identity, appearance or mannerisms or other gender-related characteristics of a person (whether by way of medical intervention or not), with or without regard to the person's designated sex at birth

intersex_status_or_sex_characteristics.definition_in_act: Intersex status - means the status of having physical, hormonal or genetic features that are: (a) neither wholly female nor wholly male; or (b) a combination of female and male; or (c) neither female nor male

areas_excluded_or_limited: Dress and appearance standards (gender identity only)

religious_organisations.summary: Broad religious exemptions allow religious organisations to discriminate against LGBTIQ+ people. For students in religious schools, protections are unclear - section 37 provides protections but these are likely overridden by broad religious exemptions in section 50(1)(c). For teachers in religious schools, discrimination is permitted but subject to procedural requirements (written policy must be provided to employees, students, parents, and public on request).

small_employers.summary: No small employer exemption in SA

other_exemptions.type: Dress and appearance standards (gender identity only)
other_exemptions.summary: Discrimination on ground of gender identity is lawful in relation to employment if the discrimination is for purposes of enforcing standards of appearance and dress reasonably required for the employment

complaint_body.name: Equal Opportunity Commission of South Australia
complaint_body.time_limit: 12 months from the act of discrimination (extensions may be granted)
complaint_body.costs_summary: No fee for lodging a complaint

statutory_basis: Equal Opportunity Act 1984 (SA), Part 4A - racial vilification only
vilification.notes: SA has vilification laws for race only. There are NO vilification protections for LGBTIQ+ people as at 31 December 2025.
vilification.source: Equal Opportunity Act 1984 (SA); Alastair Lawrie analysis

how_to_contact: Call 131 444 and ask for an LGBTI Liaison Officer
Visit your local police station and request an LGBTI Liaison Officer

key_points_summary: Sexuality, gender identity and intersex status are all protected attributes
Broad religious exemptions allow religious organisations to discriminate in most circumstances
Protection for LGBTIQ+ students in religious schools is unclear (likely not protected despite section 37)
Religious schools can discriminate against LGBTIQ+ teachers but must have written policy provided to employees, students, parents and public
Discrimination on ground of gender identity is lawful for enforcing dress and appearance standards
No vilification protections for LGBTIQ+ people (only race protected)

sexual_orientation.what_this_means: You cannot be discriminated against because you are heterosexual, gay, lesbian, or bisexual.
real_world_examples: Your employer cannot fire you for being gay, lesbian, or bisexual
A landlord cannot refuse to rent to you because you're in a same-sex relationship
A hospital cannot deny you treatment based on sexual orientation
A café cannot refuse to serve you because you're in a same-sex relationship
sexual_orientation.limitations: Asexual people are not explicitly protected. Broad religious exemptions mean religious organisations can still discriminate in many circumstances.

gender_identity.what_this_means: You cannot be discriminated against based on your gender identity, including if you are transgender, non-binary, genderfluid, or agender.
real_world_examples: Your employer must use your correct name and pronouns
You cannot be denied employment because you are transitioning or non-binary
A landlord cannot refuse housing because you are trans or non-binary
gender_identity.limitations: Non-binary protection is unclear. Employers can discriminate to enforce dress and appearance standards. Broad religious exemptions mean religious schools can likely discriminate against LGBTIQ+ students and staff.

intersex_status.what_this_means: You cannot be discriminated against based on intersex status (chromosomes, hormones, anatomy).
real_world_examples: You cannot be denied medical care based on being intersex
An employer cannot ask invasive questions about your body
You cannot be excluded from school activities because of intersex status
A gym cannot refuse you membership because you are intersex
intersex_status.limitations: Protection added in 2016. However, broad religious exemptions may allow religious organisations to discriminate.

what_youre_protected_from: Being rejected for a job because of sexual orientation/gender identity/intersex status
Being paid less than colleagues because you're LGBTIQ+
Being harassed by coworkers or managers
Being fired because you transition or come out
not_protected_from: Dress and appearance standards (employers can discriminate on ground of gender identity to enforce these)
Religious schools can likely discriminate against LGBTIQ+ teachers (must provide written policy)

what_youre_protected_from: Public schools refusing to enroll your child because you're a same-sex couple
not_protected_from: Religious schools can likely discriminate against LGBTIQ+ students (protection unclear despite section 37)

what_youre_protected_from: Landlords refusing to rent because you're in a same-sex relationship
Being evicted because of your gender identity or intersex status
Real estate agents refusing to show you properties
Being refused service at a café or restaurant
A public hospital refusing to treat you
Hotels refusing to book rooms for same-sex couples
Being excluded from a sports team because of sexual orientation or gender identity
Being denied membership in a professional association

religious_organisations.plain_english_summary: SA has broad religious exemptions. Religious organisations can discriminate against LGBTIQ+ people in most circumstances. Protection for students in religious schools is unclear.
what_they_can_do: Religious schools can likely discriminate against LGBTIQ+ students (despite section 37, likely overridden by broad exemptions)
Religious schools can discriminate against LGBTIQ+ teachers (must provide written policy to employees, students, parents, and public)
Religious organisations can discriminate in service provision
what_they_cannot_do: Religious schools must provide written policy on discrimination to employees, students, parents, and public on request
religious_organisations.real_example: A religious school can likely discriminate against LGBTIQ+ students and teachers, but must have a written policy that is provided to employees, students, parents, and available to the public on request. The protection for students is unclear - section 37 appears to protect them, but this is likely overridden by broad religious exemptions in section 50.
religious_organisations.if_you_face_discrimination: SA's protections are unclear, especially for students in religious schools. If you face discrimination, seek legal advice from Equal Opportunity Commission SA to understand if the exemptions apply to your situation.

jurisdiction_rating.rating_explanation: SA has moderate protections. Sexuality, gender identity, and intersex status are all protected. However, broad religious exemptions allow religious organisations to discriminate in most circumstances. Protection for LGBTIQ+ students in religious schools is unclear. No vilification protections. Employers can discriminate on ground of gender identity to enforce dress and appearance standards.
strengths: Sexuality, gender identity, and intersex status are all protected attributes
Religious schools must provide written policy on discrimination
gaps: Broad religious exemptions allow religious organisations to discriminate in most circumstances
Protection for LGBTIQ+ students in religious schools is unclear (section 37 likely overridden by exemptions)
Religious schools can discriminate against LGBTIQ+ teachers (with procedural requirements)
No vilification protections for LGBTIQ+ people (only race protected)
Employers can discriminate on ground of gender identity to enforce dress and appearance standards

secondary_analysis: Alastair Lawrie - A Quick Guide to Australian LGBTI Anti-Discrimination Laws
